architecture.summary: CFGzip is an offline token vocabulary compression technique that reduces the runtime overhead of context-free grammar (CFG) constrained decoding engines. It pre-computes equivalence classes of tokens that are interchangeable with respect to a given CFG, then wraps a grammar engine (XGrammar2) at inference time so the engine operates over representatives of equivalence classes rather than the full token vocabulary. At inference, sampled tokens are mapped to their equivalence class representatives before being passed to the grammar engine, and the resulting class-level mask is gathered back to the token vocabulary using a GPU-parallelizable operation. The core insight leverages syntactic congruence: if two token sequences are element-wise congruent, both are valid prefixes of the language if and only if one is.

biblio.title: Accelerating Constrained Decoding with Token Space Compression

claims.statement: latency reduction of up to two orders of magnitude when CFGzip is used with a SoTA grammar engine
up to 7.5x speedup in total constrained generation time
XGrammar2 increases inference time over the base model by factors of 2x and 10x for C++ and Bython, respectively
CFGzip cuts the overhead of XGrammar2 by one to two orders of magnitude; nearly halves the total inference time on C++, and reduces total inference time on Bython by a factor of 7.5x
CFGzip compression is provably lossless, meaning that the outputs of a grammar engine equipped with CFGzip are guaranteed to be byte-identical to those of the original engine

On Bython task, the massive improvement in both syntactic and functional correctness for all three models shows that CFG-constrained generation is critical with complex and unfamiliar context-free languages—even for 20 billion parameter models such as GPT-20B

CFGzip suffers from relatively long offline pre-computation times, which negates the advantages of the method in dynamic contexts requiring small, single-use CFGs, such as JSON schema and function call generation
